turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 15-1256, Nelson versus Colorado.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: After a conviction has been invalidated and there was no prospect of re-prosecution, may the state retain money earlier exacted from the defendant for costs, fees and restitution. In two cases decided today, Shannon Nelson and Louis Madden both paid costs, fees and restitution to the State of Colorado as a consequence of their convictions. When their convictions were overturned with no chance that they would be charged anew, both moved for refund of the amounts paid. Nelson's trail court denied her motion while Madden's post conviction court allowed the refund of costs and fees but not restitution. The same Colorado Court of Appeals Panel heard both cases and concluded that both Nelson and Madden were entitled to return of all they had paid. But the Colorado Supreme Court reversed. If held, there was only one route to refunds, Colorado's Exoneration Act. Neither Nelson nor Madden had filed a claim under that act and they had good reason not to do so. Under the Colorado's Exoneration Act, refunds are available only to a defendant who proves by clear and convincing evidence that she is actually innocent. We granted review to determine whether Colorado's scheme meets due process demands. We hold that it does not. A path marking 1976 decision, Matthews against Eldrige controls these cases. Three considerations are relevant under Matthews; the private interest affected, the risk of erroneous depravation of that interest through the procedures used and the governmental interest at stake. All three considerations weighed decisively against Colorado's scheme. First, Nelson and Madden have an obvious interest in regaining money they paid to Colorado. The state may not retain their money simply because their convictions were in place when the funds were taken. For once, those convictions were erased, the presumption of Nelson's and Madden's innocence was restored and Colorado may not presume a person adjudged guilty of no crime, nonetheless guilty enough for penal monetary exactions. Second Colorado scheme creates an unacceptable risk of the erroneous deprivation of defendant's property, Exoneration Act conditions, refunds as I just mentioned on defendant's proof of innocence by clear and convincing evidence to get their money back, defendant should not be so burdened. Once their convictions have been invalidated, they are again, presumed innocent. Moreover, the act addresses only felonies. It provides no remedy for assessments tied to invalid misdemeanor convictions. And when amounts a defendant seeks to recoup are not large, the cost of mounting a claim under the Exoneration Act and retaining a lawyer to pursue the claim would be prohibited. Third, Colorado has no legitimate interest in withholding from Nelson and Madden money to which the state currently has zero claim of right. Justice Alito has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
